coredata.dc:title: Research on correlation between corrosion resistance and photocatalytic activity of molybdenum zinc oxide modified by carbon quantum dots pigments
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Xiu-Rui
Sheng, Xiao-Xiao
Yuan, Xiao-Yu
Liu, Jin-Ku
Sun, Xiao-Wei
Yang, Xiao-Hong
coredata.dc:description: Photoelectric effect is an important and magical phenomenon in physics, so connections between photoelectricity and corrosion inhibition exist objectively. Probing the relationship between photoelectric properties and corrosion resistance is of significance but challenging. The in situ bonding modification of zinc molybdenum oxide (ZM) by carbon quantum dots (CDs) has obtained an interesting and promising result. First, the synergistic effect of protective layer shielding and photoelectron suppression effect greatly improved the corrosion resistance of CDs modified ZM (CDs@ZM) pigments. Then a positive correlation between corrosion resistance and photocatalysis of CDs@ZM pigments was first proposed. Meanwhile, based on the positive correlation between photocatalysis and corrosion resistance of pigments, a rapid method for detecting the corrosion resistance of ZM was presented. Consequently, the measurement time of corrosion inhibition was shortened.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Anticorrosion
Molybdenum zinc oxide
Carbon quantum dots
Photoelectron

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis process of the CDs@ZM pigments and epoxy composites.
SEM images of (a) ZM1. (b) CDs@ZM1. (c) ZM2. (d) CDs@ZM2. (e) ZM3. (f) CDs@ZM3 pigments.
The TG -DSC curves of (a) ZM1. (b) CDs@ZM1. (c) ZM2. (d) CDs@ZM2. (e) ZM3. (f) CDs@ZM3 pigments.
(a) The FT-IR spectra. (b) XRD pattern of pigments.
Nyquist plots for different coatings of steel after (a) 6 h immersion. (b) 24 h immersion. (c) 48 h immersion. (d) 72 h immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution.
Equivalent circuit of steel electrodes varnished with (a) epoxy resin. (b) ZM1/epoxy, CDs@ZM1/epoxy, ZM2/epoxy, CDs@ZM2/epoxy, ZM3/epoxy and CDs@ZM3/epoxy.
(a) Bode plot of steel and (b) polarization plots of steel immersed in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution containing blank, ZM1, CDs@ZM1, ZM2, CDs@ZM2, ZM3 and CDs@ZM3 coatings for 72 h.
SEM images of the steel surface coated with ZM1, ZM2, ZM3, CDs@ZM1, CDs@ZM2 and CDs@ZM3 pigments after 72 h immersion in 3.5 wt % NaCl solution.
Photocurrent transients of ZM1, CDs@ZM1, ZM2, CDs@ZM2, ZM3, and CDs@ZM3 pigments.

Photocatalytic degradation of MB solution under sunlight with different photocatalysts: (a) ZM1. (b) CDs@ZM1. (c) ZM2. (d) CDs@ZM2. (e) ZM3. (f) CDs@ZM3. (g) Broken line contrast chart. (h) UV–vis spectra. (i) Kubelka−Munk function.
Comparison diagrams of anticorrosion and photocatalytic performance of different ZM1, CDs@ZM1, ZM2, CDs@ZM2, ZM3 and CDs@ZM3 pigments.
Schematic diagram of anticorrosion mechanism of CDs@ZM.
EIS parameters of different coatings at immersion time of 72 h in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution.
Tafel parameters of different steel electrodes after immersed in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution for 72 h.

head.ce:title: Research on correlation between corrosion resistance and photocatalytic activity of molybdenum zinc oxide modified by carbon quantum dots pigments

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Advanced Materials, School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai, 200237, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering
East China University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for Advanced Materials, School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai, 200237, PR.China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Micro-Nano Powder and Advanced Energy Materials of Anhui Highter Education Institutes, Chizhou University, Chizhou, 247000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Micro-Nano Powder and Advanced Energy Materials of Anhui Highter Education Institutes

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Micro-Nano Powder and Advanced Energy Materials of Anhui Highter Education Institutes, Chizhou University, Chizhou, 247000, PR.China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photoelectric effect is an important and magical phenomenon in physics, so connections between photoelectricity and corrosion inhibition exist objectively. Probing the relationship between photoelectric properties and corrosion resistance is of significance but challenging. The in situ bonding modification of zinc molybdenum oxide (ZM) by carbon quantum dots (CDs) has obtained an interesting and promising result. First, the synergistic effect of protective layer shielding and photoelectron suppression effect greatly improved the corrosion resistance of CDs modified ZM (CDs@ZM) pigments. Then a positive correlation between corrosion resistance and photocatalysis of CDs@ZM pigments was first proposed. Meanwhile, based on the positive correlation between photocatalysis and corrosion resistance of pigments, a rapid method for detecting the corrosion resistance of ZM was presented. Consequently, the measurement time of corrosion inhibition was shortened.

ce:section.ce:para: Steel is an essential heavy metal material for social production and human life, but metal corrosion is inevitable [1–3]. There are several types of corrosion inhibition methods for metal, including barrier pigments [4,5], super hydrophobic pigments [6,7] and photocatalytic corrosion protection materials [8]. Synchronously, the photocatalytic anticorrosion protection material can use solar energy to convert photoelectrons and inhibit the corrosion of metal materials to achieve long-term anticorrosion [9,10]. In order to improve the protection efficiency, the key is to design anticorrosion pigments with photoelectric properties, in which molybdate was still potential materials.

Molybdate materials with excellent optoelectronic properties were used in many chemical fields such in metal anticorrosion pigments [11,12], fluorescent [13], electrode [14,15] and photocatalytic functional materials [16]. However, how to improve corrosion inhibition performance of molybdate pigments is still a scientific research problem. The application of anticorrosion additives can further improve the performance of metal corrosion inhibiting coatings [17–19]. To increase the protection potential of molybdate materials Mosab Kaseem et al. manufactured TiO2 modified Na2MoO4, improved the corrosion resistance of the metal by reducing the corrosion density of the composite [20]. Weihua Li et al. developed a Zn–Al layered double hydroxide with molybdate ions to inhibit corrosion of steel under neutral chloride condition [21]. For another example, molybdenum phosphate adsorbed onto the surface of anodic coating, reducing the rate of metal dissolution in acidic solution by Przemysław Kwolek et al. [22]. Previous researches confirmed the molybdate anticorrosion system. The existing corrosion inhibition mechanisms included adsorption of molybdate ions with chloride ions and formation of insoluble passivation films by molybdate and iron ions [23]. Nevertheless, the mechanism of molybdenum zinc oxide in the field of photoelectric anticorrosion has not been investigated.

Therefore, it is desirable to explore the correlation between photocatalytic properties and metal corrosion inhibition properties of molybdate materials. Due to the diversity of molybdate, various structures of molybdenum zinc oxide (ZM) pigments were synthesized. Meanwhile, in order to further improve the photoelectric properties of ZM, carbon quantum dots (CDs) were used for modification. Cheng Zhu et al. [24] indicated that CDs can slow the corrosion of iron in 1 M HCl solution. Simultaneously, the excellent up-lighting performance of CDs can effectively reduce the forbidden bandwidth of the material and enhance photoelectron longevity efficiency [25]. The experimental results showed that the corrosion inhibition and photocatalytic performance of pigments were significantly improved and positively correlated. The CDs modified ZM (CDs@ZM) pigments were expected to be developed in practical photoelectric anticorrosive materials.

Zinc nitrate hexahydrate analytical reagent (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, AR) was produced by Shanghai Titan Scientific Co., Ltd. Sodium molybdate (Na2MoO4, AR) was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Citric acid (C6H8O7, AR) and urea (CH4N2O, AR) were bought from Shanghai Ling Feng Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All the reagents were used without further purification. Epoxy resin and curing agent were produced by Changzhou ZhongPu New Material Environmental Protection & Technology Co., Ltd.

The preparation of CDs adopted bottom-up method (Fig. 1 ). 0.8 g of citric acid and 0.7 g of urea were completely dissolved in 40 mL deionized water by stirring and then heated in 100 mL teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave at 180 °C for 12 h. The product was filtered through a 0.2 μm filter, and the dialysis bag was used to retain 1000 Da molecular weight particles. Since the photoluminescence properties of dark green CDs solution, it has blue fluorescence at 365 nm UV light.

The sodium molybdate and zinc nitrate solution were prepared at 0.2 mol/L. The pH of system was adjusted to 8 using ammonia. Zn(NO3)2 solution was slowly added dropwise to Na2MoO4 solution under magnetic stirring to get white turbid liquid. Molybdenum zinc oxide was aged for 2 h and washed three times with deionized water and ethanol. Then, the ZM pigment dried in an oven at 60 °C to obtain zinc molybdenum oxide hydrate (ZM1). ZM1 was calcined at 400 and 600 °C for 2 h to obtain trizinc dimolybdate ZM2 and ZM3.

CDs@ZM pigments were prepared by in situ bonding method. 0.5 g of ZM pigment was dispersed in 30 mL of CDs solution under consecutively ultrasonic treatment for 2 h. Subsequently, the production was aged for 2 h and swilled for several times.

The preparation of epoxy composites preparation was displayed in Fig. 2 . The solid content of epoxy and epoxy resin values was 65% and 2.0, respectively. Epoxy resin coating was prepared by dispersing 0.8 g of ZM or CDs@ZM pigment in 10 g of epoxy resin by continuous mechanical stirring for 3 h. Then, 2.0 g of the curing agent was added to the epoxy coating and stirred for 20 min. Prior to coating, steel substrates of size 40 × 10 × 5 mm were sanded with 180, 240 and 600 grade SiC sandpaper and cleaned with ethanol. Next, the steel substrate was coated with epoxy mixture and weathering in air for seven days. Average thickness of the blank, ZM1, CDs@ZM1, ZM2, CDs@ZM2, ZM3 and CDs@ZM3 coatings is 85.7, 96.9, 95.0, 95.3, 95.1, 95.2 and 95.6 μm, respectively. The steel chemical composition was listed in Table 1 .

The structure of materials was characterized by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD, Shimadzu XD-3Adiffractometer) and Fourier transforms infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Shimadzu, IR Prestige-21). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Hitachi-800) was used to investigate the structures and morphologies. UV–vis spectroscopy (Shimadzu, UV-2600) was used to study the light absorption of samples. The thermogravimetric and differential scanning calorimetric (TG-DSC, 2960 SDT-TA) measurements were performed to measure the thermal stability of pigments.

The efficiency of photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue (MB) was demonstrated by UV–vis absorption data at different times. Catalysts (0.1 g) were added to 30 mL of 2 × 10 −5 g L−1 MB solution, respectively. The reaction system was stirred in dark for 0.5 h to meet the adsorption-desorption equilibrium before irradiation. The reaction was transferred to sunlight and temperature was maintained at 25 °C. Absorbance of MB solution was detected every 20 min intervals until dye became lighter over time.

The anticorrosive properties of materials were investigated on steel substances immersing in 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions for different time (6, 24, 48, 72 h) by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements. The coated steel substance was served as working electrode, saturated Hg2Cl2/Hg electrode as reference electrode and Pt sheet electrode as counter electrode. The test frequency range was from 100 Hz to 100 MHz, adopting a 10 mV amplitude perturbation. Polarization test was done from −1.4 V to −0.2 V by employing at the sweep rate of 1 mV/s.

The SEM micrographs reflected the surface morphology of ZM1, CDs@ZM1, ZM2, CDs@ZM2, ZM3 and CDs@ZM3 pigments (Fig. 3 a–f), respectively. The experimental results (Fig. 3a) indicated that the ZM1 pigment was a thin layer laminated cake structure. ZM1 pigment lost crystal water and changed its crystal structure at high temperatures. Therefore, sheet assembled flower-like structure ZM2 pigment and block stacked flower-like ZM3 pigment was obtained, respectively. The average particle sizes of ZM pigments were 2.4 μm, 2.2 μm and 2.1 μm, respectively. The particle size reduction of ZM2 and ZM3 pigments facilitated uniform dispersion in epoxy, which improved corrosion resistance of coated steel [26]. The crystal morphology of ZM pigments changed after being immersed in CDs solution. Because of the presence of N–H and C–N on the surface of CDs, the presence of hydrogen bonds between functional groups such as C–N significantly affected the growth of CDs@ZM pigments [27].

The TG-DSC analysis was carried out at static air. The curves (Fig. 4 a and b) showed a significant weight loss at 240–280 °C, while the strong exothermic peak at 269.8 °C was attributed to the loss of crystal water by ZM1 and CDs@ZM1 pigments [28,29]. Meanwhile, ZM1 weight loss of pigments was about 14.3%, which was in good agreement with the theoretical weight loss of 13.6% (Eq (1)). In TG curves (Fig. 4c–f), the pigments showed almost no weight change, which means that ZM1 will not decompose to produce gaseous substances at high temperature.
(1) Zn5Mo2O11·5H2O → Zn5Mo2O11 → Zn3Mo2O9

The FT-IR peaks of ZM and CDs@ZM pigments were all shown in Fig. 5 a, and the bond at 3440 cm−1 corresponded to O–H stretching vibration of adsorbed water [28,30]. The three FT-IR peaks ν = 705 cm−1 (Mo-Oc), 830 cm−1 (Mo-Ob) and 927 cm−1 (Mo-Ot) were attributed to characteristic vibrations of molybdate ions [11,29,31]. The peak width of the CDs@ZM pigments increased in the range of 3400–3200 cm−1, which was the result of stretching vibration of N–H [32]. Simultaneously, the peaks at 1041 cm−1 and 1384 cm−1 were attributed to C–N stretching vibration and –COOH symmetric stretching [24,33,34], which were the absorption peak characteristic of CDs. It might demonstrate that CDs have been modified with ZM by in situ bonding method.

The crystal phase structure of molybdenum zinc oxide under different synthesis conditions was analyzed by XRD ray diffraction (Fig. 5b). The intense and narrow diffraction peak indicated well crystallized of ZM pigments. Synchronously, the crystal phase structure of CDs@ZM pigments were basically the same as that of the ZM structure. All diffraction peaks of ZM1 sample prepared by precipitation method agree well with the standard card of zinc molybdenum oxide hydrate (JCPDS No: 30–1486), indicating that incorporation of CDs may not change the original crystalline structure of ZM. The crystallinity of pigment decreased and affected the growth of crystals after ultrasonic impregnation of ZM and CDs solution. The diffraction peaks of ZM2 and ZM3, which were calcined at 400 and 600 °C, were in good agreement with the standard peaks of trizinc dimolybdate (JCPDS No: 86–1771).

Nyquist diagrams (Fig. 6 ) of ZM and CDs@ZM coatings were obtained by analyzing the EIS data, revealing corrosion resistance of coating in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. A simple R (QR) model was applied to blank coating fitting circuit containing epoxy resin (Fig. 7 a), and R (Q (R (QR))) model were used for coatings that added ZM or CDs@ZM pigment (Fig. 7b). Meanwhile, R
s , R
ct , R
f , Q
ct and Q
f represent resistance to solution resistance, charge transfer resistance, film resistance, electric double layer capacitance and film capacitance, respectively [5,31,35]. Q is a constant potential element used to describe a corrosion inhibition system for non-ideal capacitive materials and the impedance was calculated as Eqs (2) and (3). In the formula, Y
0 , j, ω, and n are proportional factor, imaginary unit (j
2 = −1), angular frequency (ω = 2πf), and dispersion index (0 < n ≤ 1), respectively.
(2)
Z
Q
=
1
Y
0
( j ω )
n
(3)
Z
C
=
1
j ω
C
d l

As can be seen from Fig. 6, the impedance of CDs@ZM coatings was greater than that of ZM and blank coating. The impedance diagrams of ZM and CDs@ZM coatings were composed of capacitor loops, while the loops in low frequency section were not obvious. According to different analog circuits, different time parameters correspond to two-stage electrochemical processes. Among them, the high frequency region corresponds to the reaction of the coating/electrolyte, and the low frequency portion corresponds to the electrochemical portion of the metal/coating ring [36–38]. All the experimental datas showed circular arc shape in study area, and corrosion inhibition performance of material can be compared intuitively according to the radius of arc. After modified by CDs, the loop radius in high frequency region was significantly increased. This type of response was interpreted as shielding effect of coating on infiltration of corrosive electrolytes [39–41]. Molybdate can compete with aggressive ions to form thin liquid layer on the metal surface to reduce metal corrosion. Meanwhile, insoluble molybdate deposited at pitting site, preventing corrosion from occurring further. As immersion time prolonged, the corrosion inhibiting coating were eroded by water and corrosive ions, and impedance diagrams shrunk in diameter. Impedance drop pattern of CDs@ZM2, CDs@ZM3, CDs@ZM1, ZM2, ZM3 and ZM1 coatings were consistent with each test time point, which was the same as decrease in photocatalytic degradation performance.

The electrochemical parameters fitted by equivalent analog circuit were showed in Table 2 , which reflected corrosion resistance of coatings. After immersing in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution for 72 h, CDs@ZM pigments exhibited a higher R
ct value indicating lower electron transfer rate from metal to coating and an increase in electrical resistance [3,5]. Due to the photosensitization properties of CDs, photoelectron production efficiency is significantly improved. The photoelectrons in coating inhibited the steel loss electron corrosion process, thereby increasing R
ct value. Deposition of molybdate on surface of the steel can hinder penetration of corrosive ions [11,30]. Meanwhile, presence of electron-withdrawing groups such as C=O on surface of CDs modification material may slow down charge transfer rate [42], thereby increasing R
ct value. The peptide bond (O=C-NH) covalent bond between the CDs material and the polymer chain enables self-healing of the coating and reduces the penetration site of the corrosive medium [24].

After coated steels were immersed in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution for 72 h, the bode plot (Fig. 8 a) gave other electrochemical parameters for corrosion resistance. The electrochemical parameters at lower frequency impedance modulus (|Z| 0.1 Hz) can be attributed to interfacial process between corrosion inhibitor and steel substrate in coating [39], which slowed the electron transfer rate. Several experimental values were compared. The CDs modified ZM pigments resulted in a higher low-frequency impedance modulus due to electron suppression effect and shielding effect of corrosion inhibiting medium, thus verifying the performance improvement. The integrity of coatings can be judged by analyzing phase angle (θ) data [26,31]. The CDs modification material had a larger phase angle at low frequency end, which meant that coating had better integrity. The CDs@ZM may form protective layers and reduce penetration site of corrosive medium. As a result, the permeability of coating was reduced by self-healing of pigments, enhancing shielding properties of coating.

Fig. 8b depicted Tafel polarization curve for steel based ZM or CDs@ZM coating in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. The Tafel parameters listed in Table 3 . The addition of corrosion inhibitors in coating had inhibitory effect on anode and cathode corrosion processes. The E
corr and I
corr of epoxy coating were −889 mV and 23.67 μA cm−2, respectively. Both E
corr and I
corr were indicators that reflected corrosion inhibition effect the steel [43]. The value of E
corr became more positive, and smaller I
corr value revealed the improvement of protection efficiency. The CDs modified material had a better anode inhibition effect, which may be attributed to barrier layer molybdate product deposition at the corrosion site, reducing steel substrate electron loss. At the same time, CDs@ZM pigments were excellent optoelectronic materials. The photoelectron suppression effect can significantly reduce the corrosion current density, inhibiting occurrence of steel corrosion.

Microscopic morphology of steels coating with ZM or CDs@ZM pigments at 72 h were shown in Fig. 9 . By observing the surface samples of steel surface, the sample ZM1 showed severe corrosion, and other samples showed weaker corrosion. Unmodified ZM1 pigment not only had pitting corrosion but also cracked and peeled. The blackening and peeling of CDs@ZM coating surface were significantly reduced, which was consistent with bode low frequency analysis data of film integrity. The CDs@ZM2 coating showed the most complete surface, providing optimum protection for steel substrate through shielding and photoelectron.

The ZM2 coating exhibited a large impedance value at 72 h of 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. Simultaneously, CDs@ZM2 pigments showed the best anticorrosion effect. Significantly improved metal corrosion inhibition properties were attributed to two effects: (A) protective layer shielding effect (B) photoelectron suppression effect.
(A)
Protective layer shielding effect

In early stage of brine test, the physical shielding effect of epoxy resin coating on aggressive chloride ion and oxygen determines the corrosion inhibition effect of metal. As immersion time of steel substrates increased, the metal underwent the following anode (Eqs 4-5) and cathode reactions (Eq (6)):
(4) Fe → Fe2+ + 2e−
(5) Fe2+ → Fe3+ + e−
(6) O2 + 2H2O + 4e− → 4OH−

Molybdenum zinc oxide dissolves molybdate ions and competes with chloride ion in the electrolyte to slow the contact of the corrosive ionic steel substrate [18,44]. At the same time, molybdate and zinc ions can also react with iron ions and cathode product OH− to form an insoluble passivation film, which is deposited at corrosion site to prevent further corrosion.
(B)
Photoelectron suppression effect

A more effective way to slow down metal corrosion is to suppress the loss of electrons on steel surface to avoid oxidation reactions. ZM and CDs@ZM pigments can generate photoelectrons, and deposition of a large amount of photoelectrons on the surface of steel substrate can effectively inhibit the occurrence of corrosion. Photocurrent and photocatalytic experiments have been carried out to demonstrate generation and effectiveness of photogenerated electrons. 0.50 mol/L Na2SO4 solution was used as test system electrolyte solution. A 300 W Xe lamp with UV optical filter was employed as visible light source. A three-electrode system was used for photoresponse testing. Pt wire was used as counter electrode, Ag/AgCl electrode was used as reference electrode, and ZM or CDs@ZM pigment was used as working electrode. The photoelectric response images of ZM and CDs@ZM pigments were shown in Fig. 10 . Compared to ZM, CDs@ZM pigments had a larger photocurrent response. The results showed that the photoelectric properties of molybdenum zinc oxide materials modified by CDs can be significantly improved. To demonstrate this speculation, we conducted a photocatalytic experiment.

The production and actual effects of photogenerated electrons were visually observed by photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue (MB) solution. The photodegradation concentration of MB solution in sunlight was shown in Fig. 11 a-f. There is a significant difference in degradation effect of MB solution, indicating that the photoelectron production rate significantly affects photocatalytic performance [45,46]. The photodegradation efficiency of several materials was shown in Fig. 11g. Among them, CDs@ZM pigments have the largest number of photoelectrons and the best photodegradation of MB solution efficiency. Compared with degradation efficiency of ZM1 pigment, the degradation efficiency of CDs@ZM2 was increased by 34.2%, and degradation rate of organic dyes was 99.6% at 100 min.

The excellent catalytic of CDs@ZM material was attributed to photosensitizing properties of CDs. ZM pigment can produce photoelectrons transitions under excitation of sunlight. CDs can capture electrons from the ZM conduction band as a good electronic memory to separate electrons and holes. Typical absorption peaks (Fig. 11h) were observed at about 600 nm in CDs@ZM1, CDs@ZM2 and CDs@ZM3 samples. The peaks belong to the π-π* (CC) and n-π* (CO) absorption [24,25,47] transitions in CDs. As can be seen from Fig. 11h, the CDs@ZM pigments had a stronger UV absorption in the visible range means that utilization efficiency of light energy was increased [48]. Meanwhile, Kubelka-Munk function of ZM and CDs@ZM pigments was shown in Fig. 11i. According to the function, the band gap energies of ZM and CDs@ZM were 2.53, 2.61, 2.89, 3.11, 3.18 and 3.30 eV, respectively. The introduction of CDs significantly reduced the energy of electron transfer.

The increased electrochemical impedance values and photocatalytic efficiency comparison charts were shown in Fig. 12 . From the panoramic view of situation, the anticorrosion performance rules of molybdenum zinc oxide completely comply with the law of photocatalytic enhancement. In general, it can be visually compared that CDs@ZM has high photocatalytic ability and resistance value, among which CDs@ZM2 shows the best performance. CDs@ZM2 shows high degradation efficiency and maximum photocurrent in all pigments and the best metal corrosion inhibition performance. The consistency of experimental results proves that photogenerated electrons slow down metal corrosion by inhibiting the electron loss of steel substrates.

The inhibition mechanism of CDs@ZM can be analyzed in conjunction with Fig. 13 . Long-term protection against steel was achieved by shielding action of the molybdate corrosion inhibitor and the photoelectron inhibition. Corrosion ion penetration is prevented on account of competitive adsorption of molybdate and chloride ions. When the coating was irradiated, CDs@ZM pigments produced a mass of photoelectrons attached to steel substrate. According to the chemical balance, vast electrons on steel substrate can effectively inhibit the occurrence of steel electron-loss corrosion.

The corrosion inhibition performance of CDs@ZM2, CDs@ZM3, CDs@ZM1, ZM2, ZM3 and ZM1 pigments decrease in sequence. Remarkably, the synergistic mechanism of shielding and photoelectron inhibition was demonstrated. A positive correlation between photocatalytic performance and corrosion resistance of CDs@ZM pigments was concluded and verified. A new detection method for preliminary determination of anticorrosion performance of coating metals by photocatalytic experimental results significantly saves detection time. The modification of CDs to form CDs@ZM pigments provided a novel method for producing low-cost, highly effective corrosion resistant pigments.

Xiu-Rui Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Xiao-Xiao Sheng: Validation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Xiao-Yu Yuan: Software, Data curation. Jin-Ku Liu: Supervision, Resources. Xiao-Wei Sun: Formal analysis. Xiao-Hong Yang: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant 21878090), the Opening Project of Material Corrosion and Protection Key Laboratory of Sichuan province (Grant 2017CL15) and Key Research and Development Project of Anhui Province (201904a05020069).
